avg_down_speed_geo20.long_description: Device-weighted average fixed broadband download speed in megabits per second (Mb/s), derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly fixed-line speed test tiles spatially joined to Census 2020 block groups. Each tile's average download speed (avg_d_kbps) is weighted by the number of unique devices tested. Quarterly block group values are averaged within each year. Tract and county values are simple means of their constituent block groups.
avg_down_speed_geo20.provenance: Derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly fixed-line speed test tiles. Tiles are spatially joined to Census 2020 block groups using intersection. Per-tile average download speed (avg_d_kbps field) is aggregated to block groups using device-weighted means. Quarterly values are averaged within each year. Tract and county values are simple means of constituent block groups.
avg_down_speed_geo20.short_description: Average download speed weighted by number of devices.
avg_down_speed_geo20.statement: The average download speed is {value} Mb/s in {region_name}.

avg_up_speed_geo20.long_description: Device-weighted average fixed broadband upload speed in megabits per second (Mb/s), derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly fixed-line speed test tiles spatially joined to Census 2020 block groups. Each tile's average upload speed (avg_u_kbps) is weighted by the number of unique devices tested. Quarterly block group values are averaged within each year. Tract and county values are simple means of their constituent block groups.
avg_up_speed_geo20.provenance: Derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly fixed-line speed test tiles. Tiles are spatially joined to Census 2020 block groups using intersection. avg_up_speed_geo20.short_description: Average upload speed weighted by number of devices.
avg_up_speed_geo20.statement: The average upload speed is {value} Mb/s in {region_name}.

perc_above_25_3_geo20.long_description: Percentage of tested devices with download speed at or above 25 Mb/s and upload speed at or above 3 Mb/s, the FCC's former broadband threshold. Derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly fixed-line speed test tiles spatially joined to Census 2020 block groups. At the tile level, a tile meets the threshold if its average download speed >= 25,000 kbps and average upload speed >= 3,000 kbps. The percentage is calculated as the share of devices in qualifying tiles relative to total devices in each block group.
perc_above_25_3_geo20.long_name: Percent of devices above 25/3 Mb/s broadband threshold
perc_above_25_3_geo20.provenance: Derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly tiles. A tile qualifies if avg_d_kbps >= 25,000 and avg_u_kbps >= 3,000. The percentage is the device-weighted share of qualifying tiles within each block group. Quarterly values are averaged within each year.
perc_above_25_3_geo20.short_description: Percent of devices with speeds above 25/3 Mb/s (FCC broadband threshold).
perc_above_25_3_geo20.statement: {value}% of tested devices in {region_name} have speeds above 25/3 Mb/s.

perc_above_100_20_geo20.long_description: Percentage of tested devices with download speed at or above 100 Mb/s and upload speed at or above 20 Mb/s, a higher-tier broadband threshold suitable for multi-device streaming and large file transfers. At the tile level, a tile meets the threshold if its average download speed >= 100,000 kbps and average upload speed >= 20,000 kbps. The percentage is calculated as the share of devices in qualifying tiles relative to total devices in each block group.
perc_above_100_20_geo20.long_name: Percent of devices above 100/20 Mb/s broadband threshold
perc_above_100_20_geo20.provenance: Derived from Ookla Speedtest Open Data quarterly tiles. A tile qualifies if avg_d_kbps >= 100,000 and avg_u_kbps >= 20,000. The percentage is the device-weighted share of qualifying tiles within each block group. Quarterly values are averaged within each year.
perc_above_100_20_geo20.short_description: Percent of devices with speeds above 100/20 Mb/s (fast broadband threshold).
perc_above_100_20_geo20.statement: {value}% of tested devices in {region_name} have speeds above 100/20 Mb/s.

Age groups (under 20, 20-64, 65+) are computed by summing sex-specific single-year age variables. Both counts and percentages (relative to total population B01001_001) are provided at Census tract, county, and block group levels. Values are available on both Census 2020 and Census 2010 geography boundaries. Pre-2020 values on 2020 boundaries have been standardized using area-weighted crosswalks.

age_20_64_percent_geo20.long_description: The population estimates between age 20 and 64. 5-year ACS estimates in provided census geolevels. For non-census geolevels (such as zip codes), spatial overlaps are calculated between census block groups and target regions, then the value of each block group is aggregated to any intersecting target region based on proportion of overlap. Redistribution to non-census geolevels was performed using the redistribute R package. Values are provided on Census 2020 geography boundaries. Pre-2020 values have been standardized to 2020 boundaries using area-weighted crosswalks.

language_hh_limited_english_count_geo20.long_description: Limited English speaking household estimates from ACS 5-year estimates, table C16002 (Household Language by Household Limited English Speaking Status). A household is classified as 'limited English speaking' if no member 14 years and older speaks English 'very well.' Limited English households are computed by summing across four language groups: Spanish (C16002_004), Indo-European (C16002_007), Asian/Pacific Islander (C16002_010), and other languages (C16002_013). Both counts and percentages (relative to total households C16002_001) are provided at Census tract, county, and block group levels. Values are available on both Census 2020 and Census 2010 geography boundaries. Pre-2020 values on 2020 boundaries have been standardized using area-weighted crosswalks.

segregation_indicator_geo20.citations: Theil, H. (1972). Statistical Decomposition Analysis. Amsterdam: North-Holland Publishing Company.; Reardon, S. F., & Firebaugh, G. (2002). Measures of Multigroup Segregation. Sociological Methodology, 32(1), 33–67.
segregation_indicator_geo20.long_description: Residential segregation indicator computed as the Entropy Index (Theil's H) from ACS 5-year estimates, table B03002 (Hispanic or Latino Origin by Race). The index measures how the racial/ethnic composition of a sub-area compares to that of the state as a whole, using eight categories: Hispanic/Latino, White, Black/African American, American Indian/Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian/Other Pacific Islander, Some Other Race, and Two or More Races. Higher values indicate greater deviation from the statewide racial/ethnic composition. County and Health District values are aggregated from tract-level scores. Computed using 2020 Census geographic boundaries.
segregation_indicator_geo20.short_description: Entropy Index (Theil's H) measuring how sub-area racial/ethnic composition differs from the overall state composition.

segregation_indicator_geo20.provenance: The segregation indicator uses the Entropy Index (Theil's H), a method for measuring multigroup residential segregation that quantifies how sub-area racial/ethnic composition differs from the overall composition (Theil, 1972; Reardon & Firebaugh, 2002). This pipeline builds on methodology developed by the Virginia Department of Health, Office of Minority Health and Health Equity (VDH-OMHHE) for the Health Opportunity Index (HOI), which includes a segregation indicator as one of its 13 community health profiles.

veteran_count_geo20.long_description: Veteran population estimates from ACS 5-year estimates, table B21001 (Sex by Age by Veteran Status for the Civilian Population 18 Years and Over). Veteran count is the total veteran population (B21001_002) among civilians aged 18 and older. Percentages are calculated relative to the total civilian population 18 and over (B21001_001). Both counts and percentages are provided at Census tract, county, and block group levels. Values are available on both Census 2020 and Census 2010 geography boundaries. Pre-2020 values on 2020 boundaries have been standardized using area-weighted crosswalks.

daycare_ratio.long_description: Number of day care seats per 1,000 children under 15 years of age, which accept at least ages 4 to 10, as calculated within floating catchment areas. Catchment area ratios are based on the population of children under 15 within block groups (as estimated in the American Community Survey), capacity of eligible day care providers (as reported by the Virginia Department of Social Services), and travel times between block group centroids and day care locations (as calculated with the Open Source Routing Machine). Catchment areas are weighted by travel time with a Gaussian function with scale of 18, which are normalized for each consumer (for a 3-step floating catchment area), as calculated by the catchment R package.
daycare_ratio.long_name: Day Care Seats Per 1k Children Under 15, Accepting at Least Ages 4-10
daycare_ratio.short_description: Day care seats per 1,000 children under 15, using a 3-step floating catchment area method with Gaussian distance-decay weighting.
daycare_ratio.provenance: Day care availability ratios are calculated using the 3-step floating catchment area (3SFCA) method (Wan et al., 2012), with distance-weighted Gaussian decay.
daycare_ratio.statement: There are {value} day care seats per 1,000 children under 15 in {region_name}.

gini_index_geo20.long_description: The Gini Index is a summary measure of income inequality. The Gini coefficient incorporates the detailed shares data into a single statistic, which summarizes income dispersion across the entire income distribution. The Gini coefficient ranges from 0 (which indicates perfect equality where everyone receives an equal share) to 1 (perfect inequality where only one recipient or group of recipients receives all the income). The Gini is based on the difference between the Lorenz curve (the observed cumulative income distribution) and the notion of a perfectly equal income distribution.
gini_index_geo20.short_description: Gini Index of income inequality ranging from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality), from ACS Table B19083.
gini_index_geo20.provenance: The Gini Index is a standard measure of income inequality developed by Corrado Gini (1912). Values are computed and published by the U.S. Census Bureau in American Community Survey Table B19083.

material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.citations: Townsend, P., Phillimore, P., & Beattie, A. (1988). Health and Deprivation: Inequality and the North. London: Croom Helm.
material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.long_description: Townsend Material Deprivation Index adapted for U.S. Census data. Combines four ACS-derived indicators: (1) unemployment rate (B23025, log-transformed), (2) overcrowding — proportion of occupied housing units with more than 1 person per room (B25014, log-transformed), (3) non-car ownership — proportion of households with no vehicle (B25044), and (4) non-home ownership — proportion of renter-occupied units (S2502). Each indicator is z-scored within year and geography level, summed, the sum is z-scored again, then min-max rescaled to [0, 1]. Higher values indicate greater material deprivation. Computed using 2020 Census geographic boundaries.
material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.short_description: Townsend Material Deprivation Index (0-1) combining unemployment, overcrowding, non-car ownership, and non-home ownership.

sources.name: U.S. Census Bureau
sources.location: ACS 5-Year Estimates, Tables B23025 (Employment Status), B25014 (Tenure by Occupants per Room), B25044 (Tenure by Vehicles Available), and S2502 (Demographic Characteristics for Occupied Housing Units)

material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.provenance: Based on the Townsend Material Deprivation Index (Townsend, Phillimore & Beattie, 1988), originally developed for the UK Census to measure area-level material deprivation. This pipeline adapts the methodology for U.S. Census ACS variables. The four component indicators (unemployment, overcrowding, non-car ownership, non-home ownership) are computed from ACS 5-year estimates, z-scored within year and geography level, summed, z-scored again, and min-max rescaled to [0, 1]. The VDH Health Opportunity Index includes a similar adaptation of the Townsend index as one of its 13 indicators.
material_deprivation_indicator_geo20.statement: The material deprivation indicator in {features.name} is {value}.

employment_access_index_geo20.long_description: The Employment Access Index quantifies how easily residents of a community can reach jobs, using a gravity model that accounts for both the number of nearby jobs and the distance to them. Communities with low employment access face longer commutes, higher transportation costs, and fewer economic opportunities, all of which contribute to financial instability and reduced quality of life. This measure is a key input to the VDH Health Opportunity Index, where it contributes to the Economic Opportunity profile.
employment_access_index_geo20.provenance: Computed using a gravity model from LEHD-LODES Workplace Area Characteristics (WAC) job counts and TIGER/Line Census block centroids. Employment intensity is calculated as the sum of jobs divided by the square of the distance (in miles) from each Census block group to all nearby employment locations, using a hierarchical distance approximation: individual block-level job counts within 34 miles, tract-level aggregates from 34 to 165 miles, and county-level aggregates from 165 to 200 miles. Inspired by the Center for Neighborhood Technology (CNT) H+T Affordability Index gravity model approach.
employment_access_index_geo20.short_description: Gravity-based index of employment accessibility measuring proximity to jobs, weighted by distance decay.

sources.location: LODES WAC S000 JT00, column C000 (total jobs per Census block)
sources.name: LEHD-LODES Workplace Area Characteristics

tot_employment.long_description: Total employment counts the number of full-time and part-time jobs in a county, including wage and salary jobs plus proprietors. This measure reflects the overall size of a local economy and its capacity to provide employment opportunities for residents.
tot_employment.provenance: From Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) Regional Economic Accounts, Total full-time and part-time employment by county.
tot_employment.short_description: Total number of full-time and part-time jobs in the county, from BEA regional accounts.

tot_compensation.long_description: Total compensation measures the aggregate pay and benefits received by all employees in a county, including wages, salaries, and supplements such as employer contributions to health insurance and pension plans. This reflects the total economic value flowing to workers in a local economy.
tot_compensation.provenance: From Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) Regional Economic Accounts, total compensation of employees by county.
tot_compensation.short_description: Total compensation of employees including wages, salaries, and employer-provided benefits, from BEA.

earnings_per_job.long_description: Earnings per job measures the average compensation across all jobs in a county, combining wages, salary supplements (like employer-provided health insurance and retirement contributions), and proprietor's income. This metric reflects local economic productivity and the quality of available employment. Counties with low earnings per job may have economies dominated by lower-wage service or retail sectors.
earnings_per_job.provenance: Computed from Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) GDP and Personal Income tables. Earnings per job = total earnings / total number of jobs, where total earnings includes wages and salaries, supplements to wages and salaries, and proprietor's income.
earnings_per_job.short_description: Average earnings per job, including wages, salaries, supplements, and proprietor's income.

Child_Food_Insecurity_Rate.long_description: The percentage of food insecure children. Food insecurity refers to the US Department of Agriculture’s (USDA) measure of lack of access, at times, to enough food for an active, healthy life for all household members and limited or uncertain availability of nutritionally adequate foods. Food insecure children are those children living in households that are not necessarily food insecure all the time. Food insecurity may reflect a household’s need to make trade-offs between important basic needs, such as housing or medical bills, and purchasing nutritionally adequate foods. Geographies: County (5)
Child_Food_Insecurity_Rate.provenance: Child food insecurity estimates are produced by Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap study, which uses a regression model applied to Current Population Survey and American Community Survey data to generate county-level food insecurity rates.

access_care_indicator_geo20.long_description: Quintile indicator (1–5) measuring access to primary care, where 1 = worst access and 5 = best access. Derived from a composite z-score combining two components: (1) population-to-physician ratio, using CMS Medicare Physician PUF data to count primary care physicians (Internal Medicine, Family Practice, Pediatric Medicine, OB/GYN) within 30 miles of each census tract via OSRM driving distances and HUD ZIP-to-tract crosswalk allocation; and (2) percentage of uninsured residents aged 19-64 from ACS 5-year estimates (B27010). The underlying z-score is computed as -1 × z(z(pop_per_physician) + z(pct_uninsured)), then converted to quintiles per year across all Virginia census tracts. County and health district values are the mean of their constituent tract quintiles.

vi_overall.long_description: The Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) is a composite measure that ranks census tracts on their relative vulnerability to public health emergencies and natural disasters. It integrates 16 social factors from U.S. Census data across four themes: socioeconomic status, household characteristics, racial and ethnic minority status, and housing type and transportation. Communities with high SVI scores face greater challenges in preparing for, responding to, and recovering from hazardous events. The index helps public health officials and emergency planners direct resources to populations most likely to need support during crises.

vi_overall.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates using the CDC/ATSDR SVI 2022 methodology. Sixteen social factors from ACS tables (B/S/DP) are converted to percentages, percentile-ranked within the region (VA or NCR), summed by theme, and re-ranked. Uses B-table variables (B03002, B09001, B11012, B18101) for cross-year stability instead of DP05/DP02 which renumber across ACS vintages. Validated against CDC SVI 2020 at r>0.998 for all themes.

walkability_index_geo20.long_description: The Walkability Index is a composite measure that ranks block groups according to their relative walkability, computed annually for 2017-2023. It combines four components: (1) D2A_Ranked — employment and household land use entropy from LEHD LODES WAC job counts by NAICS sector and ACS B11001 household counts, measuring the diversity of nearby land uses; (2) D2B_Ranked — employment-only entropy across five tiers (retail, office, industrial, service, entertainment); (3) D3B_Ranked — street intersection density from the EPA Smart Location Database V3, measuring pedestrian-friendly street connectivity; and (4) D4C_Ranked — distance to the nearest transit stop computed from national GTFS feeds via the Mobility Database, measuring transit accessibility. Each component is ranked into 20 quantile bins across all block groups in the coverage area. The final index is calculated as NatWalkInd = D2A_Ranked/6 + D2B_Ranked/6 + D3B_Ranked/3 + D4C_Ranked/3, producing scores from 1 (least walkable) to 20 (most walkable). Block group scores are aggregated to Census tracts and counties using population-weighted means.
walkability_index_geo20.short_description: Composite walkability score (1-20) combining employment entropy, street connectivity, and transit proximity, updated annually from LODES, EPA SLD, and GTFS data.

sources.location: Mobility Database and Transitland GTFS feeds
sources.name: GTFS Transit Feeds

walkability_index_geo20.provenance: Derived from the EPA National Walkability Index methodology. Employment entropy (D2A/D2B) computed annually from LEHD LODES + ACS data. Street connectivity (D3B) from EPA SLD V3. Transit proximity (D4C) computed annually from national GTFS feeds. Formula: NatWalkInd = D2A_Ranked/6 + D2B_Ranked/6 + D3B_Ranked/3 + D4C_Ranked/3.

incarceration_rate_per_100000_geo20.long_description: The incarceration rate estimates how many people per 100,000 residents are held in jail, allocated to the census tracts where they lived before incarceration. Communities with high rates experience disproportionate removal of residents into the criminal justice system, which disrupts families, reduces household income, and weakens social networks. Only jail population is captured; state prison data is not available at the county level for Virginia.
incarceration_rate_per_100000_geo20.short_description: Jail incarceration rate per 100,000 residents, allocated to tracts by pre-incarceration residence.
incarceration_rate_per_100000_geo20.provenance: Tract-level incarceration counts from Prison Policy Initiative (PPI) 2020, which estimates pre-incarceration residence using Bureau of Justice Statistics survey data applied to 2020 Census group quarters. County-level annual jail population from Vera Institute (mean of quarterly observations, 2016-2023). PPI 2020 tract counts scaled by Vera year-over-year county jail population change relative to 2020 baseline. Rates computed as scaled count / PPI total population * 100,000. Virginia uses regional jails shared across counties; Vera attributes populations back to individual counties. 129 of 133 VA counties have Vera data; remaining 4 use unscaled PPI 2020 values for all years. PPI data uses 2020 census tracts natively; standardize_all appends geo suffixes.
incarceration_rate_per_100000_geo20.statement: The incarceration rate per 100,000 in {features.name} is {value}.

food_access_percentage_geo20.long_description: Food access measures the share of a tract's population that is both low-income and lives more than one mile from a supermarket. Tracts with high values have larger populations facing simultaneous barriers of poverty and geographic distance to healthy food retailers, which is associated with poorer diet quality and higher rates of diet-related chronic disease.
food_access_percentage_geo20.short_description: Share of low-income population beyond one mile from a supermarket.
food_access_percentage_geo20.provenance: Tract-level low-access, low-income population shares (lalowi1share, with lalowihalfshare as fallback) extracted from USDA Food Access Research Atlas 2015 and 2019 Excel editions. Low-income defined by Department of Treasury NMTC criteria (poverty rate >= 20%, or median family income <= 80% of state/metro median). Values for 2016-2018 linearly interpolated between 2015 and 2019 observations. Values for 2020-2023 linearly extrapolated from the 2015-2019 trend. Original FARA data uses 2010 census tracts; values crosswalked to 2020 boundaries via area-weighted redistribution using the Census Bureau tract relationship file.
food_access_percentage_geo20.statement: {value} percent of the low-income population in {features.name} lives beyond one mile from a supermarket.

Higher values indicate greater economic opportunity.

Each indicator is min-max normalized per year, inverted where higher raw values indicate worse outcomes, then combined via VDH's PCA weight matrix to produce profile scores.

health_opportunity_index_geo20.long_description: The Health Opportunity Index (HOI) is a composite index measuring social determinants of health at the census tract level. It combines 14 indicators into 4 profiles (Economic Opportunity, Consumer Opportunity, Social Impact, Built Environment) via PCA, then aggregates profiles into a single index using VDH's weight matrix. Higher values indicate greater health opportunity. Quintile labels (Very Low to Very High Opportunity) are assigned per year based on the distribution of tract-level scores.
health_opportunity_index_geo20.short_description: Composite index of social determinants of health from 14 indicators across 4 profiles.
health_opportunity_index_geo20.provenance: Reproduced from VDH HOI methodology using independently computed sub-indicators from Census ACS, LODES, EPA EJScreen, USDA FARA, CMS PUF, Vera Institute, and other sources. The 14 indicators are min-max normalized per year, inverted where higher raw values indicate worse outcomes, multiplied by VDH's 14x4 PCA weight matrix to produce 4 profile scores, then combined with composite weights to produce the final HOI score. Methodology documented in docs/composite_indices_methodology.md.

affordability_index_geo20.short_description: Proportion of income spent on combined housing and transportation costs for a Regional Typical Household.
affordability_index_geo20.long_description: The Housing + Transportation (H+T) Affordability Index measures the true cost of housing by combining housing and transportation costs as a percentage of household income. A neighborhood is considered affordable when H+T costs consume no more than 45% of household income. This measure uses the Regional Typical Household variant, which reflects a household earning the regional median income. Reproduced independently using CNT's published regression coefficients (Tables 3-6) applied to ACS demographic data, LEHD employment data, and GTFS transit schedule data at block group resolution.
affordability_index_geo20.statement: In {features.name}, combined housing and transportation costs consume {value}% of a typical household's income.
affordability_index_geo20.provenance: Independently reproduced using the methodology published by the Center for Neighborhood Technology (CNT). Regression coefficients from CNT Tables 3-6 are applied to 17 independent variables computed from American Community Survey data, LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics, TIGER geographic data, and GTFS transit feeds. Block group estimates are aggregated to tract and county via unweighted means.

environmental_hazard_index_geo20.long_description: The Environmental Hazard Index captures the cumulative burden of environmental hazards in a community by distilling 12 EPA pollution and proximity indicators into a single score. Communities with high values face greater combined exposure to air toxics, particulate matter, ozone, hazardous waste sites, and aging housing stock. The index is reported as a z-score, where zero represents the Virginia average and each unit represents one standard deviation of additional hazard burden.
environmental_hazard_index_geo20.provenance: Block-group-level data for 12 environmental indicators (CANCER, RESP, PTRAF, PWDIS, PNPL, PRMP, PTSDF, OZONE, PM25, PRE1960PCT, DSLPM, UST) extracted from EPA EJScreen annual CSVs (2016-2024, archived via Internet Archive). UST values backfilled from 2021 for years 2016-2020 when the variable was unavailable. Variables standardized per year, then reduced via Principal Component Analysis (1 component) using scikit-learn. PC1 scores converted to z-scores. Block groups aggregated to tracts via population-weighted mean (simple mean fallback). 2024 uses 10 variables (EPA dropped CANCER and RESP). EJScreen used 2010 census block groups through 2021 and switched to 2020 block groups in 2022. Pre-2022 tract values crosswalked from 2010 to 2020 boundaries via area-weighted redistribution.
environmental_hazard_index_geo20.short_description: A z-score summarizing cumulative environmental hazard exposure from 12 EPA pollution and proximity indicators.
environmental_hazard_index_geo20.statement: The environmental hazard index in {features.name} is {value}.

no_hlth_ins_pct_geo20.long_description: The uninsured rate measures the share of the population lacking any health coverage — public or private. Uninsured individuals are more likely to delay or forgo necessary medical care, leading to worse health outcomes and higher emergency department utilization. High uninsured rates often reflect gaps in employer-sponsored coverage, Medicaid eligibility, or marketplace affordability.
no_hlth_ins_pct_geo20.long_name: Percent of population without health insurance
no_hlth_ins_pct_geo20.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates, table B27010 (Types of Health Insurance Coverage by Age). Uninsured percentage = 100 × (B27010_033 + B27010_050) / (B27010_018 + B27010_034) for the working-age population (19-64). Values standardized to 2020 census boundaries using population-weighted crosswalks.
no_hlth_ins_pct_geo20.short_description: Percentage of the working-age population (19-64) without any type of health insurance coverage.
no_hlth_ins_pct_geo20.statement: {value} of the population does not have health insurance in {features.name}.

hlth_ins_pct_geo20.long_description: The insured rate measures the share of the population covered by any health insurance plan, including employer-sponsored, marketplace, Medicaid, Medicare, or military coverage. Higher insurance rates indicate better access to preventive and routine care. This measure complements the uninsured rate by highlighting areas where coverage efforts have been most successful.
hlth_ins_pct_geo20.long_name: Percent of population with health insurance
hlth_ins_pct_geo20.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates, table B27010 (Types of Health Insurance Coverage by Age). Insured percentage = 100 − uninsured percentage. Values standardized to 2020 census boundaries using population-weighted crosswalks.
hlth_ins_pct_geo20.short_description: Percentage of the working-age population (19-64) with any type of health insurance coverage.
hlth_ins_pct_geo20.statement: {value} of the population has health insurance in {features.name}.

inadequate_pc.long_description: Proportion of births where the mother received inadequate prenatal care — either starting after the fourth month of pregnancy, receiving fewer than 50% of recommended visits, or having no prenatal care at all. High inadequate rates signal systemic barriers to maternal health services such as provider shortages, lack of insurance, or geographic isolation. Inadequate prenatal care is associated with higher rates of preterm birth, low birth weight, and maternal complications. The Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) Index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index, evaluates prenatal care by combining when care began with the number of visits relative to ACOG-recommended schedules.
inadequate_pc.long_name: Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization: Inadequate
inadequate_pc.short_description: Proportion of births with late or no prenatal care, or fewer than 50% of recommended visits.

sources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

inadequate_pc.provenance: Prenatal care adequacy is measured using the Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index (Kotelchuck, M., 1994). Birth records are queried from CDC WONDER Natality Expanded (D149) by county, month, and number of prenatal visits. The index classifies each birth into four categories based on the month prenatal care began and the ratio of observed to expected visits. This pipeline assumes 39-week gestational age when computing expected visits.
inadequate_pc.statement: {value}% of births in {features.name} had inadequate prenatal care.

intermediate_pc.long_description: Proportion of births where the mother began prenatal care in the first four months but completed only 50-79% of recommended visits. Intermediate utilization often reflects barriers that prevent women from maintaining their visit schedule — transportation difficulties, work constraints, or gaps in insurance coverage — even when they initiate care early. The Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) Index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index, evaluates prenatal care by combining when care began with the number of visits relative to ACOG-recommended schedules.
intermediate_pc.long_name: Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization: Intermediate
intermediate_pc.short_description: Proportion of births with 50-79% of recommended prenatal visits, starting by the 4th month.

sources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

intermediate_pc.provenance: Prenatal care adequacy is measured using the Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index (Kotelchuck, M., 1994). Birth records are queried from CDC WONDER Natality Expanded (D149) by county, month, and number of prenatal visits. The index classifies each birth into four categories based on the month prenatal care began and the ratio of observed to expected visits. This pipeline assumes 39-week gestational age when computing expected visits.
intermediate_pc.statement: {value}% of births in {features.name} had intermediate prenatal care.

adequate_pc.long_description: Proportion of births where the mother received adequate prenatal care — starting in the first four months and completing 80-109% of recommended visits. This is the target level of prenatal care utilization. Counties with high adequate rates have maternal health systems that successfully engage pregnant women early and maintain recommended visit schedules through delivery. The Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) Index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index, evaluates prenatal care by combining when care began with the number of visits relative to ACOG-recommended schedules.
adequate_pc.long_name: Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization: Adequate
adequate_pc.short_description: Proportion of births with 80-109% of recommended prenatal visits, starting by the 4th month.

sources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

adequate_pc.provenance: Prenatal care adequacy is measured using the Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index (Kotelchuck, M., 1994). Birth records are queried from CDC WONDER Natality Expanded (D149) by county, month, and number of prenatal visits. The index classifies each birth into four categories based on the month prenatal care began and the ratio of observed to expected visits. This pipeline assumes 39-week gestational age when computing expected visits.
adequate_pc.statement: {value}% of births in {features.name} had adequate prenatal care.

adequateplus_pc.long_description: Proportion of births where the mother received more than the recommended number of prenatal visits, beginning in the first four months of pregnancy (110% or more of expected visits). High adequate-plus rates may reflect intensive monitoring of high-risk pregnancies or provider practice patterns. The Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) Index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index, evaluates prenatal care by combining when care began with the number of visits relative to ACOG-recommended schedules.
adequateplus_pc.long_name: Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization: Adequate Plus
adequateplus_pc.short_description: Proportion of births with prenatal care exceeding 110% of recommended visits, starting by the 4th month.

sources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

adequateplus_pc.provenance: Prenatal care adequacy is measured using the Adequacy of Prenatal Care Utilization (APNCU) index, also known as the Kotelchuck Index (Kotelchuck, M., 1994). Birth records are queried from CDC WONDER Natality Expanded (D149) by county, month, and number of prenatal visits. The index classifies each birth into four categories based on the month prenatal care began and the ratio of observed to expected visits (expected visits defined by ACOG guidelines). This pipeline assumes 39-week gestational age when computing expected visits, as WONDER does not report gestational age in cross-tabulations.
adequateplus_pc.statement: {value}% of births in {features.name} had adequate-plus prenatal care.

perc_children_raised_by_GPs_geo20.long_description: The share of children living in grandparent-headed households reflects family disruption driven by parental incarceration, substance abuse, military deployment, or other crises. Communities with high rates of grandparent caregiving may need additional support services including kinship care resources, legal assistance for guardianship, and financial aid for older caregivers on fixed incomes.
perc_children_raised_by_GPs_geo20.provenance: Computed from ACS 5-year estimates: table B10001 (Grandchildren Living with a Grandparent Householder) as a percentage of total population from table B01001 (Sex by Age).
perc_children_raised_by_GPs_geo20.short_description: Percentage of children being raised by their grandparents.

sources.location: 5-Year estimates, tables B10001 and B01001, via the API
sources.name: American Community Survey

perc_children_raised_by_GPs_geo20.statement: The population of children raised by grandparents is {value} in {features.name}.

median_household_income_geo20.long_description: Median household income represents the midpoint of all household incomes in a community — half earn more, half earn less. It is one of the most widely used indicators of economic well-being, reflecting local employment opportunities, wage levels, and cost-of-living dynamics. Communities with low median incomes often face compounding challenges in housing affordability, food security, and access to services.
median_household_income_geo20.provenance: From ACS 5-year estimates, table B19013 (Median Household Income in the Past 12 Months, in Inflation-Adjusted Dollars). Values are adjusted to the survey year's constant dollars by the Census Bureau.
median_household_income_geo20.short_description: Median household income in inflation-adjusted dollars.
median_household_income_geo20.statement: The median household income in {features.name} is {value} per year.

total_cropland_acres.long_description: Total cropland acreage measures the extent of land actively cultivated for crop production in a county. This indicator reflects the scale of crop agriculture and land use patterns, serving as a baseline for understanding agricultural capacity and food production potential in the region.
total_cropland_acres.long_name: Total acerage used in agriculture for cropland
total_cropland_acres.short_description: Total acreage of land under crop cultivation in the county.

sources.name: Department of Agriculture National Agriculture Statistics Service

total_cropland_acres.statement: The total acreage for cropland to {features.name} is {value}.
total_cropland_acres.provenance: USDA Census of Agriculture, county-level data for Virginia. Includes farm operations, acreage, sales, and production metrics.

total_irrigatedCropland_acre.long_description: Irrigated cropland acreage represents the portion of cropland equipped with irrigation infrastructure. Access to irrigation is a key determinant of crop yield stability and the ability to grow higher-value crops. Counties with more irrigated land tend to have more resilient agricultural output during drought conditions.
total_irrigatedCropland_acre.long_name: Total acreage used in agriculture for irrigated cropland
total_irrigatedCropland_acre.short_description: Acreage of cropland equipped with irrigation systems.

sources.name: Department of Agriculture National Agriculture Statistics Service

total_irrigatedCropland_acre.provenance: USDA Census of Agriculture, county-level data for Virginia. Includes farm operations, acreage, sales, and production metrics.

total_cropland_operations.long_description: The number of farm operations with cropland indicates how many distinct farming enterprises are engaged in crop production. This measure helps distinguish between counties where cropland is concentrated in a few large operations versus those with many smaller farms, which has implications for rural employment and economic diversity.
total_cropland_operations.long_name: Number of operations with land devoted to cropland growing
total_cropland_operations.short_description: Count of farm operations engaged in crop production.

sources.name: Department of Agriculture National Agriculture Statistics Service

total_cropland_operations.statement: The total number of operations with cropland to {features.name} is {value}.
total_cropland_operations.provenance: USDA Census of Agriculture, county-level data for Virginia. Includes farm operations, acreage, sales, and production metrics.

total_animal_expense.long_description: Total animal industry expenses capture the cost of livestock production including feed, veterinary care, and related inputs. High animal expenses often indicate a strong livestock sector and can reflect both the scale of animal agriculture and the intensity of production practices in a county.
total_animal_expense.long_name: County's total expenses towards animal industry
total_animal_expense.short_description: Aggregate expenditures on livestock production and animal care.

total_animal_expense.provenance: USDA Census of Agriculture, county-level data for Virginia. Includes farm operations, acreage, sales, and production metrics.

fruit_treeNut_total_sales.long_description: Total sales of fruits and tree nuts measures the market value of orchard and specialty crop production. This indicator highlights counties with significant horticultural activity, which tends to be labor-intensive and supports seasonal employment. Strong fruit and tree nut sales can indicate diversified agricultural economies.
fruit_treeNut_total_sales.long_name: Total sales of fruits and treenuts within a given county
fruit_treeNut_total_sales.short_description: Market value of fruit and tree nut products sold.
fruit_treeNut_total_sales.short_name: Total sales of fruits and treenuts

total_animalProducts_sales.long_description: Total animal product sales capture the market value of livestock and related products including meat, dairy, eggs, and wool. This measure reflects the economic contribution of the animal agriculture sector and is an important indicator of rural economic output and food supply capacity.
total_animalProducts_sales.long_name: Total sales of animal products within a given county
total_animalProducts_sales.short_description: Market value of all livestock and animal products sold.

total_commodity_sales.long_description: Total commodity sales represent the aggregate market value of all agricultural products sold in a county. This comprehensive measure of agricultural output captures the overall economic significance of farming and ranching, enabling comparison of agricultural productivity across counties.
total_commodity_sales.long_name: Total sales of commodities within a given county
total_commodity_sales.short_description: Aggregate market value of all agricultural products sold.

total_calves_sales.long_description: Total calf sales measure the market value of cattle calves sold in a county. Calf sales are a key indicator of the beef cattle industry, which is one of Virginia's largest agricultural sectors. This measure reflects both the scale of cow-calf operations and prevailing market conditions for beef.
total_calves_sales.long_name: Total sales of calves within a given county
total_calves_sales.short_description: Market value of cattle calves sold in the county.

total_calves_sales.statement: Total sales of calves to {features.name} is {value}.

perc_freq_mental_distress.long_description: Estimated percentage of adults reporting 14 or more days of poor mental health in the past 30 days. High prevalence of frequent mental distress reflects unmet behavioral health needs and is associated with reduced work productivity, strained social relationships, and increased demand for crisis services. Persistent mental distress at the neighborhood level often tracks with poverty, housing instability, and limited access to mental health providers.
perc_freq_mental_distress.short_description: Modeled prevalence of adults with 14+ poor mental health days per month, from BRFSS small-area estimation.

sources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

perc_freq_mental_distress.provenance: Tract-level estimates from CDC PLACES (Population Level Analysis and Community Estimates), which applies multilevel regression with poststratification (MRP) to Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) survey responses. Each model year pools two years of BRFSS data with Census tract demographics to generate small-area predictions. Values are crude prevalence (%). Confidence intervals converted from 95% CI (PLACES default) to approximate 90% MOE using the ratio 1.645/1.96.
perc_freq_mental_distress.statement: {value}% of adults in {features.name} report frequent mental distress.

perc_freq_physical_distress.long_description: Estimated percentage of adults reporting 14 or more days of poor physical health in the past 30 days. Frequent physical distress captures chronic pain, disability, and illness burden at the community level. Neighborhoods with high rates often face compounding challenges including limited healthcare access, environmental hazards, and higher proportions of residents in physically demanding occupations.
perc_freq_physical_distress.short_description: Modeled prevalence of adults with 14+ poor physical health days per month, from BRFSS small-area estimation.

sources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

perc_freq_physical_distress.provenance: Tract-level estimates from CDC PLACES, which applies multilevel regression with poststratification (MRP) to BRFSS survey responses. Each model year pools two years of BRFSS data with Census tract demographics to generate small-area predictions. Values are crude prevalence (%). Confidence intervals converted from 95% CI to approximate 90% MOE.
perc_freq_physical_distress.statement: {value}% of adults in {features.name} report frequent physical distress.
